paper_title: PhotoQuilt: Training-Free Arbitrary-Resolution Photomosaics via Bootstrapped Tiled Denoising

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Photomosaics are large images whose local regions are seen as independent tiles while their overall arrangement forms a coherent scene. Generating them at high resolution, with every tile convincing in its own right, is computationally expensive, since the canvas must hold many detailed tiles at once. We present PhotoQuilt, a training-free framework that generates photomosaics at arbitrary resolution. Diffusion models struggle to satisfy both scales at once, as direct high-resolution generation is costly and tends toward one smooth image rather than a mosaic, while patch-based tiling keeps local detail but loses global structure. PhotoQuilt resolves this with a bootstrapped tiled denoising procedure. We first produce a global composition at low resolution to fix the layout, then upscale it in latent space and re-inject noise to restore generative capacity. Denoising proceeds within fixed tiles, so each forms its own image while the shared global structure holds them in one layout. Because tile generation is handled separately, PhotoQuilt scales to large canvases without quadratic attention cost. Experiments show that PhotoQuilt outperforms current baselines on both global structure and local realism.

sections.content: 6 2 0 2 9 2 ] . [ 1 8 6 9 0 3 . 6 0 6 2 : r PhotoQuilt: Training-Free Arbitrary-Resolution Photomosaics via Bootstrapped Tiled Denoising Koorosh Roohi1,2,4 * Javad Rajabi1,2,3 * Andrew Fleet2,4,5 Babak Taati1,2,4 1University of Toronto 2Vector Institute 3Samsung Research 4KITE Research Institute 5Queens University koorosh.roohi@mail.utoronto.ca, rajabi@cs.toronto.edu Project page: https://kooroshrh.github.io/photo-quilt/ Figure 1. High-resolution photomosaics generated across different target images and tile settings. Best viewed zoomed in.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Photomosaics are large images whose local regions are seen as independent tiles while their overall arrangement forms coherent scene. Generating them at high resolution, with every tile convincing in its own right, is computationally expensive, since the canvas must hold many detailed tiles at once. We present PhotoQuilt, training-free framework that generates photomosaics at arbitrary resolution. Diffusion models struggle to satisfy both scales at once, as direct high-resolution generation is costly and *Equal contribution. tends toward one smooth image rather than mosaic, while patch-based tiling keeps local detail but loses global structure. PhotoQuilt resolves this with bootstrapped tiled denoising procedure. We first produce global composition at low resolution to fix the layout, then upscale it in latent space and re-inject noise to restore generative capacity. Denoising proceeds within fixed tiles, so each forms its own image while the shared global structure holds them in one layout. Because tile generation is handled separately, PhotoQuilt scales to large canvases without quadratic attention cost. Experiments show that PhotoQuilt outperforms current baselines on both global structure and local realism. 1 1. Introduction Mosaics are among the oldest forms of composite imagery, traditionally constructed by fitting together many small pieces of glass, stone, or ceramic of similar shape and size to form single larger picture. Photomosaics are the digital descendants of this art form, in which the small pieces are themselves independent images, whose collective arrangement reconstructs second and much larger target image [10, 18, 41]. What makes photomosaic visually compelling is that it resolves into two different images depending on the distance from which it is viewed. From up close, the eye is drawn to the detail within each individual tile, every one self-contained photograph. From afar, this tile-level detail falls away and the target image emerges in its place as coherent global scene, as shown in Fig. 1. Photomosaics appear across many fields, from industrial design and advertising to education and digital art [9]. Their meaning comes from how the tiles relate to the larger image they form, so the same technique can express very different ideas, as when photographs from persons travels come together into portrait of place they visited. This expressive power, however, comes with challenges. The same dual-scale behavior that distinguishes photomosaic from an ordinary image also makes it difficult to produce, since the target image must remain recognizable as coherent scene from afar while every tile stays convincing, meaningful image, and satisfying both at the same time is far from trivial. Apart from that, it is also computationally expensive and depends heavily on resolution, since each tile must carry enough detail to read on its own while the canvas holds the many tiles target image requires, making faithful mosaic necessarily high-resolution one and its generation correspondingly costly. Existing approaches fall into two categories. Retrievalbased methods construct photomosaic by selecting the best-matching tile from fixed image pool for each region of the target and applying local color corrections to improve the fit [14]. Because the pool is finite, tiles recur throughout the canvas, and the degree to which corrections can be applied is limited by the need to keep each tile in good quality, constraining how faithfully tile-level detail and global structure can coexist across viewing scales. Generative methods replace retrieval with synthesis, editing, or conditioning each tile on text prompt or reference image [7, 35, 52]. Diffusion models make this direction promising, but applying them to photomosaics at high resolution is non-trivial: direct generation is computationally heavy, so most methods generate tiles sequentially [9, 11], which is time-consuming and still requires additional coordination procedures to assemble independently generated tiles into coherent global image. In this work, we introduce PhotoQuilt, training-free framework that produces photomosaics at arbitrary resolution by decoupling the global composition from the generation of local tiles. Instead of generating tiles in isolation and stitching them together afterward, PhotoQuilt first establishes coarse global structure and then uses that to guide the generation of every tile. Because every tile is built on this shared foundation, the tiles fit together without separate step to align them afterward. We begin by producing the target images layout at low resolution, at very low cost. We then upscale this representation in latent space and re-inject noise. The upscaling enlarges the layout but adds no new detail on its own, so the re-injected noise gives the model the generative capacity to fill in tile-level content, developing each region of the canvas into its own detailed tile. The remaining denoising is carried out within fixed tiles, so that each tile grows into an independent, self-contained image, while the global structure fixed earlier keeps the tiles collectively aligned, ensuring that seen together they still reconstruct the target image as one coherent scene. Confining attention within each tile keeps the cost of generation linear in the size of the canvas. This per-tile confinement is what makes high-resolution mosaics practical to produce, yielding outputs in which the target image is clearly recognizable as whole while every tile remains meaningful, high-quality image in its own right, pairing that direct high-resolution generation struggles to achieve. Additionally, since each tile is denoised within its own fixed region of the canvas, the output stays true mosaic of discrete units instead of blending into single smooth image. Extensive experiments show that PhotoQuilt consistently improves both global coherence and fine-detail fidelity, outperforming existing baselines. It achieves this without any additional or specialized procedures, drawing only on the internal capabilities of the underlying model, which makes it minimal yet effective approach to high-resolution photomosaic generation that remains stable and efficient across wide range of output resolutions. 2. Related Works Photomosaics and Dual-Scale Composition. photomosaic is composed of many tile images that, viewed up close, read as distinct photographs, yet collectively reconstruct target image at distance. Classical methods [14, 41] build this by retrieving the best-matching tile from fixed pool for each block of the target and adjusting its tone [2]. This approach is inherently constrained by the pool size and limited adjustment options, which lead to repeated tiles and rigid perceptual scale. related line of work produces images that read differently at different scales or viewing angles [15, 16], though these target single image with hidden percept rather than grid of independently coherent tiles. Chung et al. [9] introduced diffusion-based generative photomosaic method, reconstructing reference image by guiding each tile toward its 2 reference block with per-step low-frequency loss to balance global structure with local detail, though it remains slow even for modest tile counts. Doyle et al. [11] take another approach, adapting text-to-image model and selecting each tiles prompt automatically from the targets colors, which keeps the method training-free. However, this method assembles the mosaic from independently generated tiles and remains costly as the tile count grows. Fast, scalable photomosaic generation on modern generative backbones thus remains an open problem. Diffusion Transformers (DiTs). Earlier text-to-image models were built on U-Net backbones, most notably Stable Diffusion [35, 39]. Modern text-to-image generation has since shifted to the diffusion transformer (DiT) [34], and rapidly growing family of open DiT foundation models now occupies this space, including SD3 [13], PixArtα [8], Sana [48], FLUX [3, 5], Qwen-Image [46], Microsoft Lens [17], and the open-weight Ideogram 4 [23]. Beyond text-to-image generation, these models increasingly support image-to-image conditioning, in which an input image steers generation through SDEdit-style re-noising [32], image-prompt or image-variation adapters [4, 50], structural control [52], or unified in-context editing [6], with several recent models such as Qwen-Image offering image-toimage generation natively. Our method treats the generator as black box requiring only text-to-image and image-toimage conditioning, making it compatible in principle with any model in this family, U-Net or DiT, and driving each tile through these interfaces via text or reference image. Diffusion Model Adaptation. Recent work has explored several ways to push pretrained diffusion models beyond their original capabilities. One direction extends the generation to resolutions larger than those seen during training. Patch-based methods denoise overlapping regions and merge them into single image. Among these, MultiDiffusion [1] jointly optimizes all patches, DemoFusion [12] combines skip residuals with dilated sampling in an upsample, diffuse, and denoise pipeline, and AccDiffusion [29] adapts text prompts to the content of each patch. Later methods improve efficiency, fidelity, and compatibility with DiT backbones [24, 26, 36, 43], while others instead modify inference-time attention or positional encodings to enlarge the models effective receptive field [19, 21, 38, 54]. Another line of work conditions pretrained diffusion models on external guidance. ControlNet [52], T2I-Adapter [33], and IP-Adapter [50] train lightweight modules to incorporate structural cues such as edge maps, depth, color palettes, or reference images, while Shum et al. [40] achieve color palette-guided generation without additional training. The photomosaic problem draws on both threads but fits neither. Like the high-resolution methods, mosaic must be generated across many tiles to cover large canvas. Yet those methods couple regions and adjust attention to suppress divergence and produce one smooth image, yet mosaic needs the opposite, since every tile must read as its own distinct image. Keeping each tile meaningful also calls for the flexible conditioning of the second thread, so tile can follow its own prompt or reference image rather than the global one. PhotoQuilt combines these needs. It reuses patch-wise generation but lets tiles diverge instead of converge, and conditions each tile on its own. 3. Method We turn pretrained text-to-image model into photomosaic generator with no training and no architectural change. At the core of our method, there is simple idea. single coarse latent is shared across the whole image to fix what it looks like at the large scale, and is then completed independently inside each tile. The result is an image that works at two scales, the target image when seen as whole and separate, self-contained image within every tile. On top of this formulation we expose single conditioning interface with two independent controls. The first sets the global target, which can be generated image or real one. The second sets what each tile shows, which by default is the global prompt but can instead be tile-specific prompt or an image drawn from reference gallery. The same procedure scales to high resolutions and large tile counts (Sec. 4). 3.1. Preliminaries Diffusion Models. Modern text-to-image models generate images in compressed latent space by progressively evolving samples from pure-noise Gaussian distribution toward target data distribution through sequence of intermediate distributions, process governed by continuous time parameter [0, 1]. pretrained encoder maps pixel image to latent representation and decoder inverts this mapping, so that all generation operates in latent space. For clean latent z0 and noise ϵ (0, I), the intermediate latent at time is zt = αt z0 + σt ϵ, [0, 1] (1) where the schedule coefficients αt and σt are chosen so that the path runs from the clean latent z0 at t=0 to pure noise ϵ at t=1. Different choices of αt and σt recover different formulations, such as variance-preserving diffusion [20, 42] and flow matching [13, 30, 31]. In our derivation we adopt the linear schedule αt = 1 and σt = with t=0 the clean latent and t=1 pure noise. network vθ(zt, t, c) is trained to regress the velocity ϵ z0 under condition c, and sampling runs this process backward in to recover clean latent from noise. We denote by Φ(cid:0)zinit; ta tb, c(cid:1) (2) 3 the operation that runs this backward process from ta to tb, starting at zinit under condition c. Partial renoising. Given clean latent z0, the standard SDEdit [32] operation lets us partially corrupt it with noise and then recover clean latent again, rather than starting from pure noise. strength (0, 1) controls how far this corruption goes. Using Eq. (1), we mix z0 with noise up to time t=s, and then integrate the backward process from t=s back down to t=0 to obtain new clean latent. Tiled denoising. common way to generate large images is to split the latent into spatial tiles, denoise each independently, and recompose them, an approach introduced for patch-wise generation [1]. At high resolution, independently denoised tiles often show repetition, so prior work lets tiles influence one another, for instance via shared residuals or interleaved sampling [12, 29]. We deliberately avoid this cross-tile coupling, since our goal here is different. We want each tile to develop independently. 3.2. Problem Formulation and Overview photomosaic has deliberate two-scale structure. At the fine scale, it is grid of distinct, self-contained images; at the coarse scale, these tiles together reconstruct single target image. We formalize this two-scale structure with two criteria. Given global condition g, either base prompt c0 or reference image I0, set of tiles {Ωk}K k=1, and tile conditions {ck}K k=1, we seek an image mosaic at the target resolution such that: (i) Global Reconstruction. The low-frequency content of mosaic reconstructs the target structure specified by g. (ii) Tile Autonomy. Each tile (Ωk) is complete, selfcontained image consistent with ck. We achieve these two criteria with two separate mechanisms. single coarse latent, shared across all tiles and renoised once, fixes the low-frequency layout they have in common, giving us global reconstruction. Each tile is then denoised as its own independent trajectory from that shared latent, giving us tile autonomy. 3.3. Bootstrapped Tiled Denoising Globally-Coherent Initialization. We first obtain base latent zbase at low resolution, either by generating it from the base prompt (c0) or by encoding provided image (I0), zbase = (cid:40) Φ(cid:0)ϵlow; 1 0, c0 E(I0), (cid:1), (generated base) (image base) (3) with ϵlow (0, I) and as the encoder. We upsample that to the target latent grid using fixed upsampler U, then renoise it once with strength s, ˆz = U(cid:0)zbase(cid:1), zs = (1 s) ˆz + ϵ, ϵ (0, I) (4) 4 Figure 2. The PhotoQuilt method pipeline. Pixel space representation has been used instead of latent space for simplicity. Because < 1, the renoised latent zs retains the coarse content of ˆz while leaving its high-frequency detail to be regenerated. As zs is shared by every tile, it fixes common low-frequency layout across the image. The strength thus acts as single global/local control: smaller enforces the global structure more strictly, while larger grants each tile more freedom to diverge. Independent Per-Tile Denoising. We split the target latent into equal, non-overlapping tiles {Ωk} and denoise all tiles together, each as separate trajectory. Each tile starts from its own region of the shared renoised latent and follows its own condition ck, (cid:16) z(k) 0 = Φ zs (cid:12) (cid:12)Ωk ; 0, ck (cid:17) , = 1, . . . , (5) Algorithm 1 PhotoQuilt: bootstrapped tiled denoising tile conditions {ck}K k=1; k=1; fixed E, D, Eq. (3) upsample to target grid Eq. (4) Require: global condition g; strength s; partition {Ωk}K Ensure: photomosaic image mosaic 1: zbase (Φ(ϵlow; 1 0, c0) or E(I0)) 2: ˆz U(zbase) 3: zs (1 s) ˆz + ϵ, ϵ (0, I) 4: for all {1, . . . , K} (parallel) do (cid:12) zs (cid:12)Ωk ; 0, ck (cid:16) z(k) 0 Φ 5: 6: end for 7: return mosaic {z(k) (cid:17) (cid:17) (cid:16) 0 }K k=1 Eq. (5) Eq. (6) (cid:12) (cid:12)Ωk where zs runs at native resolution per tile. restricts the shared latent to tile Ωk; denoising Final Photomosaic. We now decode the denoised global latent, consisting of independently denoised tiles, Figure 3. Photomosaic (12k 6k) generated from base image (4k 2k) using real images as tiles condition. Best viewed zoomed in. 64 64 resolution using PSNR, SSIM [45], LPIPS [53], HPSv2 [47], and Image Reward [49]. Tile quality is evaluated on individual tiles using CLIP score [37], BLIP [28], CLIP-IQA [44], HPSv2 [47], and Image Reward (IR) [49], capturing prompt alignment and quality of each tile. mosaic = D(cid:0) {z(k) 0 }K k= (cid:1) (6) 4.2. Baseline Methods Tile independence in Eq. (5) is what makes the output mosaic rather than an upsampled image. By default, tiles do not overlap, so the seam between tiles are obvious. When smoother, continuous appearance is preferred, the tiles can be given small overlap and blended where they meet, which removes visible seams. This is optional and not used in our main results. See Fig. 2 for the PhotoQuilt pipeline. 4. Experiments 4.1. Experimental Setup evaluate Implementation Details. We the modelagnostic PhotoQuilt across three backbone configurations: Stable Diffusion 2.1 (SD2.1) [39], FLUX.1 [3], and FLUX.2 [5]. The SD2.1 backbone is included to enable fair comparison with competing methods that are constrained to this backbone. All methods operate on mosaics of resolution 6144 6144 with tile size 768 768 (8 tiles per axis), matching the native generation resolution of SD2.1. To ensure consistent global structure across all evaluated methods, the base image for every method is generated at 768 768 using FLUX.1 from the same prompt, giving all methods an identical starting point. Bicubic interpolation has been used as the upscaling function. To construct our evaluation set, we selected 12 prompts from the Aesthetic-4K [51] dataset that highlight distinct photomosaic characteristics, pairing each with 15 random seeds to generate 180 unique samples. Metrics. We evaluate global structure preservation and tile quality with complementary metric families. local Global structure is evaluated by comparing downsampled version of the full mosaic against the shared base image at We compare PhotoQuilt against six baselines. Match & Tone [14, 41] is the classical retrieval pipeline: each block is matched to fixed image pool and its tone is adjusted to the target, serving as our non-generative lower bound. AdaIN [22] transfers the color and style statistics of each target block onto an independently generated tile, lacking explicit structural conditioning. Color T2I-Adapter [33] injects block-level color map via an adapter network as spatial conditioning signal during tile denoising. NoiseBlend [27] combines each tiles initial noise with crop of the target latent at fixed ratio, approximating our shared initialization but without explicit renoising. StreamDiff [25] processes all tiles as high-throughput batch under the same prompt with no global coordination. Phomosaic [9] guides SD2.1 tile generation with per-step low-frequency structural loss and AdaIN-style color alignment. 4.3. Evaluation Quantitative results. Results are shown in Table 1. key observation cuts across all baselines: global fidelity and local tile quality are in tension, and no prior method resolves both simultaneously. StreamDiff achieves the highest PSNR and SSIM among baselines by processing all tiles under the same image-to-image stream, but this comes at the cost of tile diversity as tiles fail to diverge and instead produce near-identical content, which collapses local CLIP, BLIP, and preference scores. The color-conditioning methods (Color T2I-Adapter, AdaIN, Phomosaic) maintain reasonable tile-level scores but sacrifice global structure fidelity, as they transfer only low-level color statistics to each tile rather than adapting tile content to the spatial region it must reconstruct. Match & Tone achieves moderate local 5 Figure 4. Qualitative comparison of photomosaic generation. Compared to baselines, PhotoQuilt better preserves the global target structure while generating realistic, self-contained tiles. 6 Table 1. Quantitative evaluation of global structure preservation (64 64 downsampling) and local tile quality. HPS denoting HPSv2, IQA denoting CLIP-IQA, and IR denoting Image Reward. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined.

sections.title: Local Tiles
sections.content: PSNR SSIM LPIPS HPS IR CLIP BLIP IQA HPS IR 17.58 20.90 10.78 15.46 25.83 20.98 25.61 30.15 19. 0.35 0.55 0.12 0.33 0.84 0.57 0.78 0.89 0.63 0.33 0.23 0.56 0.34 0.05 0.24 0.08 0.04 0.19 0.13 0.15 0.13 0.21 0.22 0.16 0.23 0.23 0. -2.28 -2.19 -2.28 -0.60 -0.11 -2.16 0.05 0.09 -0.05 0.34 0.34 0.33 0.18 0.23 0.34 0.35 0.36 0.34 0.76 0.84 0.74 0.08 0.19 0.87 0.88 0.93 0. 0.78 0.62 0.88 0.25 0.37 0.79 0.87 0.79 0.87 0.20 0.19 0.21 0.08 0.10 0.21 0.21 0.23 0.24 -0.60 -0.46 -0.69 -2.23 -2.05 -0.35 -0.35 0.21 0. scores, since its tile pool is drawn from FLUX.1-generated images, but falls short on global metrics due to the rigidity of retrieval. NoiseBlend, without explicit renoising, degrades on both axes. PhotoQuilt breaks this trade-off across all tested backbones. On SD2.1, it outperforms Phomosaic in global structure while matching or exceeding its tile quality. Critically, this improvement stems from content-level coordination rather than simple color transfer; the shared renoised base provides spatially-aware initialization, forcing tiles to adapt their distinct content to fit the global target  (Fig. 4)  . Furthermore, our training-free procedure is backbone-agnostic and scales directly with model quality: PhotoQuilt (FLUX.1) achieves the highest CLIP and IR scores alongside strong global metrics, while the FLUX.2 variant posts the strongest overall HPSv2 and BLIP scores. Qualitative results. Fig. 4 compares methods on three prompts spanning different global structures and conditioning modes. AdaIN and Phomosaic preserve approximate color distributions per tile but produce tiles whose content is largely independent of the global spatial arrangethe zoomed insets reveal that individual tiles are ment; plausible images disconnected from the surrounding structure. StreamDiff reproduces the global image faithfully but at the cost of tile autonomy: tiles are near-copies of the region, making the mosaic read as single blurry image at close range rather than grid of distinct photographs. Our method, produces tiles that are simultaneously selfcontained images and spatially-coherent contributors to the global composition, property clearly visible in the zoomed insets for all three scenes. Fig. 3 showcases PhotoQuilt generating photomosaic using real image as the base and gallery of real images as tile conditions. 4.4. Ablation Study We ablate three design choices of PhotoQuilt on the FLUX.1 backbone, reported in Table 2. All configurations use the 6144 6144 target resolution with 64 tiles. Global guidance (bootstrap). Removing the bootstrapped initialization entirely and replacing the shared renoised latent with independent per-tile noise, maximize local tile quality: BLIP reaches 1.00 and Image Reward improves by +0.94, as each tile is free to denoise from scratch toward its condition without any structural constraint. However, global structure collapses completely, with PSNR dropping to 13.10 and SSIM to 0.11, confirming that the shared renoised base is the sole source of layout coherence. The tiles are individually high-quality but collectively reconstruct no global target. Renoising strength. The strength (0, 1) controls what fraction of the full denoising trajectory is reserved for tile-level generation: small injects little noise and preserves the coarse latent almost intact, while large approaches full renoising and grants each tile nearcomplete generative freedom. The ablation sweeps {0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8} and reveals clean monotonic trade-off, as shown in Fig. 5. At = 0.2, global fidelity peaks (PSNR 42.55, SSIM 0.99) but tiles are forced to complete an almost clean latent, leaving little room to diverge; local scores degrade sharply, with Image Reward falling to 2.27. Increasing relaxes this constraint: at = 0.8, tile-level Image Reward rises to 0.76 but global SSIM falls to 0.62. The default = 0.6 sits at the crossover where both criteria are simultaneously well-satisfied, achieving the best balance between layout fidelity and tile independence. This confirms that is meaningful and smoothly-varying dial between criterion (i) and criterion (ii), as described in Sec. 3.3. Bootstrap resolution. We vary the resolution of the base image generated in the first stage before upsampling, testing 256 256, 512 512, and the default 768 768. At 256 256 the global structure collapses entirely (PSNR 9.88, SSIM 0.03), indicating that small bootstrap retains too little spatial detail for the tiles to recover the global lay7 Figure 5. The default configuration (s = 0.6, 768 768) provides the optimal balance between global layout fidelity and local tile realism compared to the ablated variants. Table 2. Ablation study on the FLUX.1 backbone evaluating the impact of renoising strength (s) and base image (bootstrap) resolution on photomosaic generation. Configuration Global Structure (64 64) Local Tile Quality PSNR SSIM LPIPS Default (s = 0.6, 7682) 30.15 0.89 0.04 Renoising Strength (Bootstrap = 768 768) HPS 0.23 IR 0.09 CLIP BLIP 0. 0.93 IQA 0.79 HPS 0.23 IR 0.21 = 0.2 = 0.4 = 0.8 42.55 (+12.40) 36.55 (+6.40) 22.71 (-7.44) 0.99 (+0.10) 0.97 (+0.08) 0.62 (-0.27) 0.00 (-0.04) 0.00 (-0.04) 0.24 (+0.20) 0.25 (+0.02) 0.24 (+0.01) 0.18 (-0.05) 0.59 (+0.50) 0.54 (+0.45) -1.49 (-1.58) 0.16 (-0.20) 0.31 (-0.05) 0.37 (+0.01) 0.02 (-0.91) 0.68 (-0.25) 0.97 (+0.04) 0.38 (-0.41) 0.50 (-0.29) 0.85 (+0.06) 0.08 (-0.15) 0.15 (-0.08) 0.26 (+0.03) -2.27 (-2.48) -1.24 (-1.45) 0.76 (+0.55) Bootstrap Size (s = 0.6) Bootstrap = 256 256 Bootstrap = 512 512 9.88 (-20.27) 29.03 (-1.12) 0.03 (-0.86) 0.87 (-0.02) 0.52 (+0.48) 0.04 (0.00) 0.08 (-0.15) 0.23 (0.00) -2.28 (-2.37) -0.05 (-0.14) 0.16 (-0.20) 0.36 (0.00) 0.02 (-0.91) 0.92 (-0.01) 0.29 (-0.50) 0.78 (-0.01) 0.07 (-0.16) 0.23 (0.00) -2.27 (-2.48) 0.18 (-0.03) No Global Guidance Without Bootstrap 13.10 (-17.05) 0.11 (-0.78) 0.54 (+0.50) 0.12 (-0.11) -2.28 (-2.37) 0.37 (+0.01) 1.00 (+0.07) 0.88 (+0.09) 0.29 (+0.06) 1.15 (+0.94) out. At 512 512 the degradation is mild (PSNR 1.12, SSIM 0.02). The full 768 768 bootstrap consistently provides the richest structural guidance and is the default for all reported results. Together, these results show that bootstrap quality propagates directly into global fidelity: higher-resolution base encodes more spatial detail into zs, giving each tile more informative initialization of its region of the global image. 5. Conclusion We presented PhotoQuilt, training-free framework for generating photomosaics at arbitrary resolution. Instead of generating tiles in isolation and combining them afterward, it decouples global composition from local generation through bootstrapped tiled denoising, fixing coarse layout at low resolution, upscaling it in latent space, and renoising it once so that single shared latent enforces global structure while each tile denoises as its own trajectory. Since tiles are generated separately, the method scales to large canvases without quadratic attention cost. Requiring no training or architectural change, PhotoQuilt applies to both UNet and DiT backbones, outperforming existing baselines on both global structure and local realism. One remaining challenge is that in image gallery conditioning mode, reconstruction quality depends on the diffusion backbone, since denoised tile may diverge from its reference image.

sections.title: Supplementary Material
sections.content: A. Implementation Details All experiments were executed on NVIDIA H100 GPUs. Where official source code for baseline methods was unavailable, we faithfully re-implemented the algorithms according to the technical specifications provided in their original publications. For our generative backbones, we utilized the following specific model checkpoints: FLUX.1-Krea-dev-12B for the FLUX.1 [3] evaluations, FLUX.2-Klein-9B for FLUX.2 [5], and Manojb/stable-diffusion-2-1-base for Stable Diffusion 2.1 [39]. B. Tile and Base Conditioning The dual-scale formulation of PhotoQuilt exposes highly flexible conditioning interface with two independent axes: the global base condition and the local tile conditions. This allows the framework to operate in several distinct generative modes without altering the underlying bootstrapped denoising procedure. Base (Global Structure). The global structure of the photomosaic is established in the first stage and can be driven by either text or vision. When generating purely novel scene, the base is conditioned on global text prompt c0. Alternatively, to reconstruct specific real-world target, the base can be initialized from an arbitrary reference image I0. In this latter case, I0 is encoded directly into the latent space using the backbones specific encoder to serve as the structural anchor, ensuring the low-frequency layout faithfully matches the real target. Tiles (Local Content). During the independent per-tile denoising phase, each tile is guided by its own condition ck, has its own separated condition tokens positioned at each tiles origin point. PhotoQuilt supports three primary modes for tile-level conditioning: 1. Shared Global Prompt: By default, ck = c0. Even when all tiles share the same global text condition, the independent denoising trajectories and the restricted attention mask force the tiles to diverge into distinct, selfcontained images that collectively respect the target layout. 2. Per-Tile Text Prompts: For fine-grained semantic control, each tile can be guided by unique text prompt ctile , allowing explicit control over the subject matter of individual tiles. high resolution sample of different prompts for global image and tiles is shown in Fig. S1. 3. Image Gallery Conditioning: To replicate the classical retrieval-based photomosaic experience using generative AI, tiles can be conditioned on real images drawn from provided gallery = {g1, . . . , gM }. We assign gallery entry to each tile via mapping function π : {1, . . . , K} {1, . . . , } that samples uniformly at random from the gallery. The tile is then conditioned through the backbones native image interface, ck = R(cid:0)gπ(k) (cid:1), (7) where represents the image conditioning adapter (Redux [4] for FLUX.1, and the built-in image-to-image conditioning for FLUX.2). This recovers the classical tile-pool setting, but uses each reference as generative guidance rather than pasted patch. Consequently, the synthesized tiles adopt the semantic and stylistic characteristics of the reference images while structurally adapting to fit their region of the shared base latent. An example in high resolution is shown in Fig. S2. C. Further Global Structure Evaluation Table S1 extends our global fidelity analysis to 32 32, 128 128, and 256 256 downsampling scales, capturing both coarse layout fidelity and mid-frequency structure preservation. While StreamDiff [25] posts strong pixellevel scores across all resolutions, this strictly stems from its failure to produce independent tiles, collapsing the mosaic into single continuous image. True mosaic baselines (Phomosaic [9], AdaIN [22], Match & Tone [14, 41]) show significant structural degradation at finer scales (128 128 and 256 256), confirming that mere color transfer cannot enforce rigid spatial alignment. PhotoQuilt demonstrates robust structural preservation across all evaluated resolutions. The FLUX.1 variant consistently achieves the highest PSNR, SSIM, and lowest LPIPS at every scale, proving that our bootstrapped initialization tightly binds the global layout even as the evaluation resolution increases. Furthermore, PhotoQuilt (FLUX.2) dominates perceptual alignment (HPSv2 and Image Reward) at the most challenging 128 128 and 256 256 scales, underscoring its capacity to maintain complex spatial compositions across the entire canvas. More qualitative comparison is shown in Fig. S3. D. Inference Time Analysis Table S2 details the inference times of PhotoQuilt and the evaluated baselines. While Match & Tone reports the lowest inference time, this figure exclusively represents the matching and tone-adjustment phase; as retrieval approach, Table S1. Quantitative evaluation of global structure preservation across multiple downsampling scales (3232, 128128, and 256256). Local tile metrics are omitted for clarity. HPS denotes HPSv2, and IR denotes Image Reward. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined. Inference time comparison of photomosaic generation methods at 6144 6144 resolution.

sections.title: Method
sections.content: Inference Time (s) Match & Tone AdaIN Color T2I-Adapter NoiseBlend StreamDiff Phomosaic PhotoQuilt (SD2.1) PhotoQuilt (FLUX.1) PhotoQuilt (FLUX.2) 61.09 284.17 527.34 346.74 87.57 267.22 97.15 209.59 139.41 it relies on pre-computed pool of images generated via FLUX.1, the substantial computational cost of which is excluded from this measurement. Despite this, PhotoQuilt operating on the SD2.1 backbone achieves speeds highly competitive with this retrieval lower bound. StreamDiff similarly posts fast execution times using the SD2.1 backbone by processing tiles in an uncoordinated batch. However, as established in our main evaluation, this high-throughput pipeline critically compromises mosaic quality by collapsing tile diversity. PhotoQuilt achieves comparable generation speeds on the same backbone while strictly preserving both global layout fidelity and local tile autonomy. The algorithmic efficiency of our bootstrapped tiled denoising approach becomes most apparent when compared to Phomosaic, our primary fully generative competitor. As shown in Table S2, when operating on the identical SD2.1 backbone, PhotoQuilt significantly outpaces Phomosaic, demonstrating that our per-tile denoising mechanism is fundamentally faster than relying on iterative alignment losses and separate coordination steps. More notably, this architectural efficiency allows our method to comfortably scale to state-of-the-art architectures: PhotoQuilt remains faster than Phomosaics SD2.1 implementation even when executing on the heavier FLUX.1 and FLUX.2 DiT backbones. E. Multi-GPU Distributed Generation for Ultra-High-Resolution Photomosaics key advantage of PhotoQuilts tile-level denoising is its natural compatibility with distributed generation across multiple GPUs. This enables the synthesis of ultra-highresolution canvases (e.g., 14k14k) without compromising quality or requiring approximation (see Fig. S4). Unlike standard global-attention diffusion models where every token attends to the entire image, our method confines generation to fixed-size, spatially local attention windows. This allows us to partition the upscaled global latent into horizontal bands split cleanly along tile-row boundaries. Each band is assigned to separate GPU holding full model replica, allowing denoising to proceed in parallel with equal load balancing. To guarantee that this distributed execution perfectly matches single-GPU run, we enforce trajectory the timestep shift (µ) in the FLUX denoisconsistency: ing schedule is computed once based on the full-canvas sequence length and shared across all replicas. This prevents individual bands from calculating localized shift and drifting onto divergent denoising paths. Beyond denoising, the final pixel-space decoding via the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) presents severe memory bottleneck; natively decoding even full-width strip of massive canvas easily exceeds standard GPU memory capacities. To make peak memory independent of the total canvas size, PhotoQuilt employs two-dimensional, distributed block-tiling strategy for the decode phase. The latent is divided into small two-dimensional blocks, and these blocks are distributed round-robin across the GPUs and decoded. This yields an ultra-high-resolution output whose maximum scale is bounded only by aggregate host memory, transforming generation into purely throughputlimited process. Figure S1. Photomosaic (8192 8192) generated with text prompts while the text prompt for the tiles (512 512) is different than the global image. 14 Figure S2. Photomosaic (12288 6144) generated from base image (4096 2048) using real images as tiles (256 256) through image gallery conditioning. The gallery has been fetched from web and the base image has been encoded then upscaled to the preferred resolution. 15 Figure S3. Photomosaic (8192 8192) generated with text prompts while the text prompt for the tiles (512 512) is different than the global image. Figure S4. Photomosaic (14336 14336) generated using the multi-gpu distribution feature with 256 256 tiles. base image has been used, and the text prompt for tiles was bird. The generation has been done using 4xH100 GPUs on single node. Best viewed zoomed in.
